planting.note: Plant acorns immediately after collection in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep loamy to sandy soil in full sun; sawtooth oak is one of the fastest-growing oaks and is widely planted in wildlife food plots across the southeastern US for its heavy, early acorn production — a full crop typically beginning in 5–8 years from planting.

directions: Plant acorns immediately after collection in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep loamy or sandy soil in full sun; sawtooth oak is an Asian oak (native to China, Korea, and Japan) widely planted throughout the southeastern and mid-Atlantic US as a fast-growing wildlife tree, producing large, chestnut-like acorns in heavy crops beginning at 5–8 years of age — far earlier than most native oaks. The long, narrow, chestnut-like leaves (reminiscent of chestnut rather than typical oak foliage) turn golden yellow in fall. The large acorns are eagerly consumed by deer, turkey, wood ducks, and other wildlife. Its fast growth rate, adaptability, and early, heavy acorn production make it the most widely planted non-native oak for deer and wildlife habitat planting in the Southeast. However, it can naturalize and potentially displace native oaks in some regions — use thoughtfully near natural areas.

planting.note: Plant acorns immediately after collection in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, fertile to average loamy soil in full sun; English oak is widely adaptable to a range of soil types and is tolerant of clay and somewhat wet conditions, unlike many oaks. Grows more slowly than American red oak but lives for centuries.

directions: Plant acorns immediately after collection in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep fertile to average loamy soil in full sun; English oak is the archetypal oak of European culture — the 'royal oak,' the great forest tree of England, the sacred tree of the Celts — producing the classic, deeply lobed oak leaf and small, elongated acorns held on distinctive long stalks (peduncles, giving the alternate common name 'pedunculate oak'). English oak has been planted throughout North America in parks, estates, and arboreta for centuries, developing into magnificent specimens in suitable climates. It is more tolerant of clay soils and seasonal wetness than most American oaks. The acorns are consumed by squirrels, jays, deer, and other wildlife. Many cultivated varieties exist, including the fastigiate (columnar) 'Fastigiata' or 'Skyrocket' forms widely used in street tree plantings. A very long-lived tree — English oaks commonly reach 500–800 years of age, and individual trees are counted among the oldest living things in Europe.

planting.note: Plant acorns in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy, dry to average soil in full sun; holm oak is a Mediterranean species adapted to hot, dry summers and mild winters — it thrives in the California and Pacific Northwest coastal climates but is marginal in the humid Southeast. Extremely drought tolerant once established.

directions: Plant acorns in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy, dry to average soil in full sun; holm oak is the iconic evergreen oak of the Mediterranean Basin — a massive, dark, brooding, evergreen tree with small, dark green, holly-like leaves (hence both 'holm' from Old English 'holly' and the species epithet ilex meaning 'holm oak'). It forms the characteristic dark green groves and monte bajo of southern Europe and North Africa. In California, holm oak has been planted for over a century and shows excellent adaptation to the Mediterranean climate — drought-tolerant, long-lived, and providing deep, dense shade year-round. The acorns are small and bitter, but the related Holm oak variety (Quercus ilex subsp. ballota) from Portugal and Spain produces sweet, edible acorns eaten raw or roasted — the famous Spanish 'bellota' acorn eaten by Ibérico pigs. An excellent large evergreen tree for hot, dry climates in zones 7–10.

planting.note: Plant acorns in fall in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; cork oak is native to the western Mediterranean and requires excellent drainage, moderate to low fertility, and dry summer conditions. It tolerates moderate drought and salt spray but not wet, heavy clay soil.

directions: Plant acorns in fall in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; cork oak is the source of virtually all wine and champagne cork in the world — the extraordinarily thick, spongy, deeply furrowed outer bark is harvested every 9–12 years from mature trees (a process called 'cork stripping') that does not harm the tree, as the bark regenerates completely. Portugal and Spain produce about 80% of the world's cork. The tree is an evergreen with a broad, spreading canopy and small, evergreen, slightly holly-like leaves. First cork harvest is typically at age 25 years; subsequent harvests every 9–12 years for the remaining 150–200 years of the tree's life. In California, cork oaks have been grown for over a century as ornamentals and heritage trees, thriving in the Mediterranean climate. An interesting tree with historical and economic significance for home gardeners in zones 7–10.

planting.note: Plant acorns in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile, loamy or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; Chinese evergreen oak is one of the most ornamental and adaptable evergreen oaks for zones 7–9, with excellent heat and humidity tolerance and a refined, upright form. More shade-tolerant than most oaks.

directions: Plant acorns in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile, loamy or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; Chinese evergreen oak is a handsome, moderate-sized evergreen oak native to China, Japan, and Korea, producing long, narrow, slightly wavy-margined, glossy dark green evergreen leaves with distinctive reddish-bronze new growth — creating a refined, almost tropical appearance more reminiscent of a Southern Magnolia relative than a typical oak. One of the most ornamental and well-behaved evergreen oaks for humid subtropical gardens in zones 7–9, more tolerant of the heat and humidity of the Southeast than many evergreen oaks from the Mediterranean. The slender leaves and upright, oval crown make it suitable for smaller garden spaces where live oak would be too massive. Produces small, elongated acorns. Increasingly available at specialty nurseries and becoming recognized as an excellent evergreen street and garden tree for the Southeast.
